title: useEventListener with proper cleanup, type inference, and ref-target
scenario_context: Custom hook to add event listeners with TS inference per event type, attached to window/document/element. Naive implementations leak listeners on rerender or pass stale handlers.
verification_chain: Layer 8: hook accepts target (window/document/element ref) and infers EventMap
Layer 8: handler ref updated each render so cleanup not needed per handler change
Layer 9: events fire with latest handler closure
Layer 10: listener_count gauge in dev mode catches leaks
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Adding listener with handler in deps — adds/removes per render
Forgetting passive: true for scroll/touch — jank
No SSR guard — window undefined error
Handler captures stale state via closure
what_breaks_naive_approach: Adding mousedown listener in useEffect with [handler] deps; parent re-renders every keystroke; listener added/removed every keystroke; profile shows GC pressure.
reasoning: Pattern: useRef holds latest handler. useEffect with [eventName, target] only — adds listener once. Listener invokes ref.current. Works for window, document, and element refs via union type. SSR-safe by checking typeof window.
backend_solution: // N/A

error_handling_strategy.telemetry: Dev: count active listeners per type

edge_cases_addressed: SSR — no window
Ref starts null then attaches — re-runs when ref.current changes? Use callback ref pattern if needed
options object changes identity each render — memoize at call site
passive option for scroll/touch
Capture phase
Multiple listeners same element same event
Unmount mid-event
test_strategy: Vitest with jsdom. Click event fires latest handler after handler prop changes. Unmount removes listener.
observability_hooks: Dev-mode listener tracker for leak detection
what_distinguishes_senior_thinking: 1) Handler in ref — no churn on each render. 2) Overloaded types per target — autocomplete shows correct event types. 3) SSR-safe by default.

title: useClickOutside that handles portals, nested click targets, and mouse vs touch
scenario_context: Dropdown menu closes on outside click. Naive document.addEventListener('click') misses portal children (rendered outside DOM tree) and fires before inner clicks bubble correctly.
verification_chain: Layer 8: hook accepts ref, fires callback when outside
Layer 8: respects portals via composedPath check
Layer 9: mousedown + touchstart for instant close on mobile
Layer 10: drop_outside_total event
anti_patterns_to_avoid: click event — fires after blur, missing input changes
Checking event.target.contains(ref.current) — wrong direction
Not handling Shadow DOM
No touchstart — sluggish on mobile
what_breaks_naive_approach: Date picker portal renders into document.body. Click inside picker — naive code thinks it's outside (not within ref.current subtree) — closes immediately.
reasoning: Use mousedown (fires before click → before button click handler) + touchstart. Check event.composedPath() (handles Shadow DOM, portals via React's manual portal stack). Allow array of refs for compound widgets. Optional 'ignore' selector list for known portal containers.
backend_solution: // N/A
edge_cases_addressed: Portals — composedPath includes them
Multiple refs (toggle button + menu) — array support
Touch — touchstart
Iframes — composedPath crosses Shadow root only, iframes blocked by same-origin
Disabled state
Nested useClickOutside — outer fires first via mousedown ordering
Right-click — only left button
test_strategy: jsdom click outside ref → fires. Click ref → no fire. Click portal element passed in ignore → no fire.
what_distinguishes_senior_thinking: 1) mousedown not click — closes before downstream button clicks. 2) composedPath handles portals + shadow DOM. 3) Multiple-ref support for compound widgets.

title: useMediaQuery using matchMedia listener API with SSR fallback
scenario_context: Responsive components branch on viewport. window.matchMedia listener API (addEventListener) is more efficient than resize + check. SSR must produce same markup as first client paint to avoid mismatch.
verification_chain: Layer 8: hook returns boolean for query
Layer 8: useSyncExternalStore for tear-free updates
Layer 8: SSR returns explicit fallback to prevent hydration mismatch
Layer 10: query_match_change_total per query
anti_patterns_to_avoid: window.innerWidth in render — read on every render
Resize listener with throttle — wakes JS for every resize
matchMedia.addListener (deprecated) — use addEventListener
Returning undefined on SSR — flicker on hydrate
what_breaks_naive_approach: Mobile menu hidden on desktop via window.innerWidth > 768 in render. SSR renders desktop (no window) → mismatch warning + reflow.
reasoning: matchMedia returns MediaQueryList with addEventListener('change', ...). Hook uses useSyncExternalStore so concurrent rendering doesn't tear. SSR fallback is explicit param. The right default avoids both layouts being shown — pick the larger one for SSR if unsure (CSS handles small via media queries until JS catches up).
backend_solution: // N/A
edge_cases_addressed: SSR/CSR mismatch — explicit fallback
Browser without matchMedia — fallback
Multiple components same query — each subscribes (could share via cache)
Query string typo — matchMedia returns matches:false
prefers-color-scheme — same hook
prefers-reduced-motion — important for animations
Print media query — works
test_strategy: jsdom mock matchMedia. Toggle match, assert re-render.
what_distinguishes_senior_thinking: 1) useSyncExternalStore for concurrent safety. 2) SSR fallback parameter — explicit avoids hydration mismatch. 3) Modern addEventListener — deprecated API avoided.

title: useIdleTimeout with grace period, activity tracking, and tab visibility
scenario_context: SaaS app auto-logs out after 15 min idle. Naive: simple setTimeout. Misses: cross-tab activity, visibility (don't log out a backgrounded tab user actively in another tab), grace period for save-in-progress.
verification_chain: Layer 7: idle state separate from inactivity
Layer 8: BroadcastChannel syncs activity across tabs
Layer 9: 30s grace period with warning toast + 'Stay signed in'
Layer 10: idle_timeout_triggered_total, idle_grace_recovered_total
anti_patterns_to_avoid: setTimeout single — drift after suspend/wake
Tracking activity in every component — leak
No grace period — user mid-typing kicked
Logout on visibility change — penalizes split-screen users
what_breaks_naive_approach: User in another tab actively for 14min — original tab times out, logs them out. With BroadcastChannel, activity in any tab refreshes idle in all.
reasoning: Track 'last activity' via mousemove/keydown/touch + BroadcastChannel from other tabs. setInterval checks elapsed every 1s. At threshold, enter 'grace' state — show warning. After grace, fire onIdle. Visibility hidden + active in another tab counts as active.
backend_solution: // N/A

error_handling_strategy.server_errors: Logout API failure → force local logout
error_handling_strategy.user_facing_messages: 'Session expiring in 30s' toast with stay-signed-in button
error_handling_strategy.recovery_actions: Any activity or button click cancels grace

edge_cases_addressed: Multiple tabs — BroadcastChannel sync
System sleep/wake — drift caught by interval re-check
Browser throttles inactive tab — accept slightly delayed logout
Grace period — user reads warning
ReducedMotion — no toast animation
Service worker — separate keepalive
Cross-origin embeds — own channel scope
test_strategy: Vitest fake timers; idle then activity. Multi-tab BroadcastChannel mocked.
observability_hooks: idle metrics — adjust threshold based on user behavior
what_distinguishes_senior_thinking: 1) BroadcastChannel cross-tab activity. 2) Grace period preserves user trust. 3) Visibility/system sleep handled via wall-clock check.

title: useAsync with AbortController, ref-tracked latest, and explicit reset
scenario_context: Generic async operation runner. Form submit, data fetch, etc. Needs cancellation, latest-only result (not stale), status state machine, and reset.
verification_chain: Layer 7: discriminated state — idle/pending/success/error
Layer 8: only latest call's result honored
Layer 9: explicit reset for re-runnable operations
Layer 10: async_operation_duration{name}
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Stale closure on success setter
No abort — slow request overwrites fast retry
Returning data + error simultaneously
useEffect-only — no manual trigger
what_breaks_naive_approach: Slow request then fast retry — slow finishes second, overwrites fast result with stale.
reasoning: requestId counter; only finalize state if requestId matches latest. AbortController per call. Reset returns to idle. Hook returns {state, execute, reset}.
backend_solution: // N/A
edge_cases_addressed: Concurrent execute calls — only latest counts
Unmount mid-execute — abort + state ignored
reset during pending — abort
throw inside fn after abort — silently ignored
Sync errors in fn — caught by try
Args change between calls — closures fresh
React strict mode double-invoke safe
test_strategy: Vitest: latest-call wins. Abort on second call. reset returns idle.
observability_hooks: Wrap execute in caller for timing metrics
what_distinguishes_senior_thinking: 1) requestId guards against late results. 2) AbortController threaded to fn. 3) Discriminated union state.

title: Async-handler wrapper that forwards rejections to express error middleware
scenario_context: Express 4 (and many Express 5 codebases) drop async route errors silently — unhandled rejections crash process or get logged generically. Wrapper ensures every async route fwds to errorHandler.
verification_chain: Layer 4: every route uses asyncHandler
Layer 4: error middleware sees structured errors
Layer 10: unhandled rejection alarm proves wrapper coverage
anti_patterns_to_avoid: try/catch in every route — boilerplate
Forgetting to catch in async middleware
process.on('unhandledRejection') as a safety net for known cases
Returning res.json from inside catch — handles errors twice
what_breaks_naive_approach: async route throws DB error — Express 4 doesn't catch → falls into Node's unhandled rejection → process eventually crashes or error swallowed.
reasoning: asyncHandler wraps handler in Promise.resolve(fn(req,res,next)).catch(next). Linter rule (or RegisteredRouter wrapper) ensures all async routes use it. Express 5 catches automatically, but the wrapper is still useful for compatibility and explicitness.
frontend_solution: // N/A

error_handling_strategy.server_errors: Errors thrown async are forwarded to error middleware automatically
error_handling_strategy.recovery_actions: Process exits cleanly on unhandled rejection

edge_cases_addressed: Sync throw inside async — caught by Promise.resolve
Error in middleware after res.json — express handles
Express 5 — wrapper is no-op but harmless
next() called twice — error middleware idempotent
Streaming response — error mid-stream destroys response
Timer-based async (setTimeout) — wrapper doesn't help, use AbortController
Worker thread rejections — separate listener
test_strategy: Integration: async route that throws → error middleware fires.
what_distinguishes_senior_thinking: 1) Auto-wrap router avoids forgotten asyncHandler. 2) Final unhandledRejection handler exits — fail fast over silent corruption. 3) Express 5 awareness.

title: Sentry/Rollbar integration with PII scrubbing and breadcrumbs
scenario_context: Sending errors to Sentry but accidentally leaking emails, tokens, credit cards in request payloads. PII violation. Need scrubber + breadcrumbs for context.
verification_chain: Layer 4: Sentry initialized with PII scrubbers
Layer 10: each request sets user, tenant, route context
Layer 10: sensitive fields redacted in beforeSend
Layer 10: sentry_events_total{level}
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Sentry.init without beforeSend — full request body leaks
Logging req.body before scrub
User PII in event message text
Stripping nothing in dev — habits matter
what_breaks_naive_approach: Login API throws 500. Request body {email, password} sent to Sentry. Anyone with Sentry access sees plaintext password. SOC2 violation.
reasoning: beforeSend hook in Sentry init: walk event tree, redact known sensitive keys (password, token, ssn, credit_card, etc.) and authorization headers. Set user context via scope.setUser({id, tenantId}) — no email/name. Breadcrumbs auto-include console/fetch/navigation — also need PII scrub via beforeBreadcrumb.

error_handling_strategy.server_errors: All caught errors → Sentry with scrubbed context

edge_cases_addressed: Nested PII in deeply nested objects — recursive redact
Headers map with mixed case — toLowerCase compare
Replay sessions — maskAllText + blockAllMedia
Source maps uploaded — error stacks readable but no source on client
User explicitly opts out — disable via consent
Errors from third-party libs — same redaction
Performance traces — sample rate keeps cost down
test_strategy: Unit test redact recursion. Integration: throw error with PII in body → Sentry test mode → assert no PII.
observability_hooks: Sentry releases tagged via env. Replay sample rate adjustable.
what_distinguishes_senior_thinking: 1) Recursive redactor with depth limit. 2) Replay masking enabled by default. 3) User context without PII (id only).

title: Toast vs banner vs modal — severity-based error UX
scenario_context: Errors come in severities: transient (network blip), persistent (validation), critical (subscription canceled). One UI doesn't fit all. Toasts for transient, banners for persistent, modals for blocking critical.
verification_chain: Layer 8: ErrorPresenter component picks by severity
Layer 8: banners persist until dismissed/resolved
Layer 8: modals block interaction
Layer 9: toasts auto-dismiss with action button
Layer 10: error_dismissed_total{severity, kind}
anti_patterns_to_avoid: All errors as toasts — critical messages missed
All as modals — UX hostility
No action affordance — user can't recover
Multiple toasts stack indefinitely — overwhelming
what_breaks_naive_approach: User on past_due plan tries to invite member. Toast says 'failed'. They don't see it (scrolled). They keep trying. Modal interrupts: 'Subscription past due, update payment' — they understand and act.
reasoning: ErrorPresenter chooses by severity + kind. severity: info/warning/error/critical. kind: transient/persistent/blocking. Toasts: short, transient (network retry). Banners: persistent (validation, plan limits). Modals: critical blocking (auth expired, payment required). Each carries action.
edge_cases_addressed: Modal stacks — only one at a time
Toast limit — max 3 visible
Banner conflict — newest replaces same-kind
Action callback throws — caught
Screen reader — modals trap focus, toasts aria-live
Mobile — modal full-screen
Critical override — banner can be promoted to modal
test_strategy: Storybook: trigger each code, assert correct UX form. Unit: presentError dispatches correctly.
observability_hooks: Dashboard: which errors most-shown, dismissed-without-action rate
what_distinguishes_senior_thinking: 1) UX form matches severity — junior code uses one form for all. 2) Server hints via meta — backend knows context. 3) Default actions per code — recovery built-in.

title: Discriminated unions across API responses for explicit success/failure
scenario_context: API endpoint returns either success data or business error (e.g., signup might 'succeed with email verification needed' or 'succeed with auto-login'). Two-status union > status code branching.
verification_chain: Layer 5: response is union with 'kind' discriminator
Layer 6: client narrows on kind, full TS support
Layer 8: component switches exhaustively
Layer 10: outcome_total{endpoint, kind}
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Returning data + optional verificationRequired flag — error-prone
HTTP 200 with success:false in body — confusing
Different shapes per branch with no discriminator
Boolean flags multiplying
exhaustive checks via comments not types
what_breaks_naive_approach: Signup returns {user, token?} where token absent means 'verify email'. Devs forget the check, log in with no token, get 401, debug forever.
reasoning: Response always {kind: 'signed_in', token, user} | {kind: 'verification_required', email} | {kind: 'pending_approval', adminEmail}. Client switches on kind. TS forces exhaustive handling. Each kind has only relevant fields.

error_handling_strategy.server_errors: Errors thrown as before (4xx/5xx). Discriminated union is for business outcomes.
error_handling_strategy.recovery_actions: Verification sent → resend; pending → notify when approved

edge_cases_addressed: New kind added — TS forces frontend to handle
Old client meets new kind — Zod safeParse fallback to 'unknown' UX
Server adds field to a kind — backward-compat as long as old fields stay
API versioning — kind list scoped per version
Testing — exhaustive switch unit tested
Migration — old endpoints kept while new clients adopt
Documentation — generated from schema
test_strategy: TS exhaustiveness test (default: never assertion). Integration: each path triggered, returns correct kind.
what_distinguishes_senior_thinking: 1) Discriminated unions force exhaustive handling. 2) Each kind only has relevant fields — no nulls/optionals lying. 3) Zod runtime validation catches drift.

title: Per-tenant rate limiting with token bucket and Redis
scenario_context: Public API per-tenant. Tenant A bursts 10k requests/sec, starves tenant B. Need per-tenant rate limit honoring plan tier, with proper 429 + Retry-After.
verification_chain: Layer 3: rate limiter checks before service
Layer 4: middleware applies limit per tenant + endpoint class
Layer 5: 429 with Retry-After header and reset epoch
Layer 6: client respects Retry-After
Layer 10: rate_limit_hit_total{tenant, endpoint_class}
anti_patterns_to_avoid: IP-based limit — multiple tenants behind NAT
In-memory counter — multi-instance inconsistency
Fixed window — burst at window boundary
Generic 'rate limited' message — no retry info
what_breaks_naive_approach: Tenant A burst denies tenant B. Or: misconfigured limit too strict — legitimate users blocked. Per-plan limits + token bucket smooth bursts within plan.
reasoning: Token bucket: capacity (burst) + refill rate (sustained). Redis script atomically decrements and computes wait time. Plan tier maps to limits. Headers: X-RateLimit-Limit, X-RateLimit-Remaining, X-RateLimit-Reset, Retry-After. Client SDK respects Retry-After before retry.
shared_schema: // shared/schemas/rateLimit.ts\nimport { z } from 'zod';\nexport const RateLimitInfoSchema = z.object({ limit: z.number(), remaining: z.number(), resetAt: z.coerce.date(), retryAfterSeconds: z.number().optional() });
edge_cases_addressed: Burst tolerance via capacity
Redis down — fail open with warning (or closed if strict)
Plan upgrade mid-request — next request uses new limit
Endpoint classes (read/write/expensive) — separate buckets
Cross-region — Redis cluster
Test mode bypass via header (server-side env check)
Spot user excess — graph spike on dashboard
test_strategy: Token bucket lua tested with fake clock. Integration: burst within capacity allowed, exceed → 429 + retry-after.
observability_hooks: Per-tenant rate hit dashboard. Alert: tenant > 90% of limit sustained — upsell opportunity.
what_distinguishes_senior_thinking: 1) Token bucket > fixed window — handles bursts gracefully. 2) Per-plan limits + endpoint class — fair multi-tenancy. 3) Client respects Retry-After — server isn't hammered.

title: Plan change with proration and clear preview
scenario_context: User upgrades mid-cycle. Stripe prorates automatically; user expects clear preview ('$X today, $Y next month'). Naive: just call subscription update, user sees mysterious invoice.
verification_chain: Layer 3: preview proration via stripe.invoices.retrieveUpcoming
Layer 4: preview endpoint returns immediate charge + next-cycle total
Layer 5: change endpoint requires user confirmation token from preview
Layer 8: confirm modal shows breakdown
Layer 10: plan_change_preview_total, plan_change_committed_total
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Updating subscription without showing prorated amount
Allowing downgrade mid-cycle that loses paid features immediately
No undo within trial window
Hardcoded plan IDs in frontend
what_breaks_naive_approach: User clicks upgrade. Card charged $400 (prorated). User confused — expected $50/mo. Refund request. With preview, they see '$X today, $Y/month going forward, you're saving $Z'.
reasoning: Stripe's invoices.retrieveUpcoming with subscription_proration_behavior='create_prorations' returns the prorated invoice. Frontend shows it. User confirms. Server applies same change with the upcoming invoice's preview as a 'confirmation token' (hash of relevant params) to prevent price-tag stealing.

error_handling_strategy.server_errors: CONFLICT if preview token mismatch (price changed)

edge_cases_addressed: Concurrent change by another admin — preview token catches
Price change in Stripe between preview and commit — token mismatch
Downgrade to grandfathered plan — separate path
Annual to monthly — proration of unused annual
Currency change — explicit warning
Cancellation during change — block
Trial conversion — handled separately
test_strategy: Stripe test mode: preview, change price, commit fails, re-preview, succeeds.
what_distinguishes_senior_thinking: 1) Preview before commit — no billing surprises. 2) Preview token prevents stale-price commits. 3) Line-item breakdown earns trust.

title: Subscription state machine: trial → active → past_due → canceled
scenario_context: Subscription has many statuses with distinct UX. Naive: render all features for any non-canceled. Need explicit state machine with feature gating per state.
verification_chain: Layer 1: status enum + state transition log
Layer 3: state changes via webhooks; explicit transition guards
Layer 4: middleware blocks operations based on state
Layer 8: per-state UX (trial banner, past-due banner, canceled lock screen)
Layer 10: state_transition_total{from, to}
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Boolean isActive flag — loses nuance
Allowing creation when past_due — accrues unpayable revenue
Hard delete on cancel — user can't reactivate
No grace period at state boundaries
what_breaks_naive_approach: Past_due tenant keeps creating projects. Bills accumulate. When they finally pay or cancel, dispute. With state-aware middleware, read continues but writes blocked with clear message.
reasoning: Status: trialing → active → past_due → unpaid → canceled (also: incomplete, trialing-expired). Each transition logged with timestamp + cause. Middleware checks state per operation: reads allowed in past_due; writes denied. UI per state: trial=countdown, active=normal, past_due=banner+CTA, unpaid=read-only, canceled=reactivate flow.

error_handling_strategy.server_errors: BillingError with specific meta per state
error_handling_strategy.recovery_actions: Per state: update payment, reactivate, choose plan

edge_cases_addressed: State change webhook race with operation — operation re-checks
Trial end at midnight UTC — boundary handling
Reactivate after canceled — keeps data + history
Manual admin state change — audit logged
Failed reactivation card — past_due immediately
Multi-tenant ownership change — sub follows new owner
Dispute reversal returns to active
test_strategy: Each transition tested. Write attempts in each state. Banner UX per state.
what_distinguishes_senior_thinking: 1) Explicit state machine > boolean. 2) Read/write asymmetry — past_due users can still see data. 3) Per-state UX with recovery action.

title: Team invitation with email verify, token expiry, and seat enforcement
scenario_context: Admin invites by email. Invitee receives email, clicks accept link. Naive: no expiry, no seat check, accept creates user without password setup.
verification_chain: Layer 1: invitations table with token, expiresAt, status
Layer 3: invite checks seat count vs plan limit; sends email via job
Layer 4: accept-by-token endpoint validates token + expiry
Layer 5: token in URL is signed (HMAC) so tampering detected
Layer 8: accept screen prompts password set + welcome
Layer 10: invite_sent, invite_accepted, invite_expired metrics
anti_patterns_to_avoid: No token expiry — old links stay valid
Auto-create account without password — security risk
No seat check at invite time — seat full after acceptance
Sequential or guessable tokens
Acceptance grants access before email confirmed
what_breaks_naive_approach: Admin invites 5 people, plan allows 3 seats. All 5 accept, 5 users created, billing breaks. With invite-time seat check + accept-time re-check, denied gracefully.
reasoning: Invite: validate seat capacity (plan limit - current users - pending invites). Token = signed JWT or random UUID with HMAC + 7-day expiry. Email contains link. Accept: validate token signature + expiry + status; re-check seats (may have been used since invite); prompt password + accept terms; mark invite consumed.

error_handling_strategy.server_errors: NotFound for invalid/expired; Quota for seat exhausted
error_handling_strategy.user_facing_messages: 'Invite expired — ask your admin to resend'

edge_cases_addressed: Token tampered — HMAC fails
Invite expired — UI shows clear message
Invite consumed — second click shows 'already accepted'
Email already a user in another tenant — separate flow (cross-tenant invite)
Admin revokes invite — token rejected
Plan downgrade reduces seats below current — existing users keep access, no new invites
Email typo — admin resends to correct address
test_strategy: Token verification unit. Integration: invite, accept, second accept fails, expired invite fails.
what_distinguishes_senior_thinking: 1) HMAC token — tamper-proof without DB lookup on every check. 2) Seat re-check at accept time — race-safe. 3) Explicit password set + terms — security + compliance.

title: Conditional UI rendering that mirrors server-side permissions exactly
scenario_context: UI shows admin actions to admins. Naive: client checks role string. Drift: server adds new permission gate, UI doesn't update.
verification_chain: Layer 8: <Can> component checks via permissions context
Layer 8: context loaded from /me/permissions on auth
Layer 9: stale permissions auto-refresh on focus
Layer 10: ui_action_attempted_total{action, allowed}
anti_patterns_to_avoid: role === 'admin' check in component
Hardcoded action lists
Showing disabled button — invites questions
Not refreshing on role change
what_breaks_naive_approach: Backend adds 'billing:manage' permission. UI still shows billing tab to all. User clicks, gets 403.
reasoning: Server emits ROLE_GRANTS shared via schema. /me/permissions returns user's effective grants. <Can> hides UI for missing actions. Refresh on focus catches role changes. UI is convenience; server is enforcement.
backend_solution: // see e2e-008

error_handling_strategy.server_errors: 403 if client UI permits but server denies (drift)

edge_cases_addressed: Role change mid-session — focus refresh
Loading state — render no actions until perms loaded
Permissions hook used in many places — single context
Per-resource permissions (own vs any) — Can takes 'on' prop
Test mode override
SSR — perms not available, render nothing
Suspend/wake — focus event fires
test_strategy: Storybook with each role. Drift test: client allows, server denies.
what_distinguishes_senior_thinking: 1) Server is source of truth, UI mirrors. 2) Focus refresh catches role changes. 3) <Can> hides not disables — fewer confused users.

title: Recharts custom tooltip with rich content and proper portal placement
scenario_context: Tooltip needs to show multi-metric values, deltas, and links. Default tooltip is text-only and overflows container.
verification_chain: Layer 8: custom Tooltip component receives payload
Layer 8: portal to body avoids overflow clipping
Layer 9: tooltip dismisses on click outside
Layer 10: tooltip_view_total
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Tooltip inside ResponsiveContainer with overflow:hidden — clipped
Tooltip with links — Recharts hides on hover-out before click
Default tooltip — limited customization
Tooltip blocks chart click handlers
what_breaks_naive_approach: Tooltip with 'View detail' link disappears before user can click it. Use sticky tooltip toggled by click.
reasoning: Custom Tooltip component renders content from payload. For interactive content, switch to active mode triggered by click rather than hover. Use a Portal to avoid clipping. Pin/unpin pattern.
backend_solution: // N/A
edge_cases_addressed: Tooltip near edge — flip side
Mobile (no hover) — tap to show, tap outside to hide
Multiple charts — single tooltip at a time
Reduced motion — no animation
RTL layout — flip
Touch + hover hybrid — show on touchstart
Print — hide tooltip
test_strategy: Storybook with edge positions. Click link, navigate.
what_distinguishes_senior_thinking: 1) Portal avoids clipping. 2) Click-pin for interactive tooltips. 3) Position aware of viewport edges.

title: Visx treemap with hierarchical drill-down and breadcrumb
scenario_context: Cost breakdown visualization: cloud spend by service → resource type → resource. Treemap navigation through hierarchy.
verification_chain: Layer 6: API returns nested hierarchy
Layer 7: state tracks current focus node
Layer 8: visx Treemap with click handlers per node
Layer 9: breadcrumb back; keyboard arrow nav
Layer 10: drill_event_total{depth}
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Loading entire 100k-leaf tree client-side — heavy
No back navigation — user stuck
Treemap without labels — cryptic
Color encoding without legend
what_breaks_naive_approach: Tree is huge, loading all 100k nodes; first render 8s.
reasoning: Server returns one level at a time. Treemap renders current node's children. Click drills in. Breadcrumb tracks path. Keyboard: arrow keys cycle, Enter drills, Esc backs out.
shared_schema: // shared/schemas/hierarchy.ts\nimport { z } from 'zod';\nexport const HierarchyNodeSchema: z.ZodType<any> = z.lazy(() => z.object({ id: z.string(), name: z.string(), value: z.number(), children: z.array(HierarchyNodeSchema).optional() }));
edge_cases_addressed: Single child — auto-drill or render
Very deep tree — breadcrumb collapses with ellipsis
Zero-value nodes — filtered
Equal-value siblings — visual stability
Keyboard nav — tabindex + arrow keys
Reduced motion — no transition
Screen reader — labels include value
test_strategy: Storybook with mock hierarchy. Drill + back tested.
what_distinguishes_senior_thinking: 1) Lazy loading per level — handles big trees. 2) Breadcrumb + keyboard. 3) Visx + d3-hierarchy where Recharts can't.

title: Chart.js mixed chart (bar + line) with shared axis and synced legend
scenario_context: Sales chart: bars for monthly revenue, line for cumulative. Two y-axes. Need: shared x, two y-axes scaled appropriately, legend toggles series.
verification_chain: Layer 8: chart config with two scales, two datasets
Layer 8: legend toggles individual datasets
Layer 9: hover shows both values at same x
Layer 10: chart_legend_toggle_total
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Both series same axis — line dwarfed or stretched
Default colors only — color-blind issues
No legend — user can't tell which is which
Update via new Chart() — leak
what_breaks_naive_approach: Revenue 0-50k, cumulative 0-500k on same axis; revenue bars 1px tall.
reasoning: Chart.js scales config: yLeft for bars, yRight for cumulative. position: 'right' on right axis. Datasets specify yAxisID. Legend onClick handler toggles dataset. Use chart.update('none') for legend toggles.
backend_solution: // N/A — same as e2e-019
edge_cases_addressed: Empty data — chart shows empty axes
Negative cumulative — beginAtZero false
Single month — bars still render
Very different scales — two axes
Color-blind palette
Tooltip mode 'index' shows both
Print — chart visible
test_strategy: Storybook with various data. Resize test. Update test.
what_distinguishes_senior_thinking: 1) Two y-axes — series readable. 2) update('none') on data change — no animation flicker. 3) Destroy on unmount.

title: ECharts geographic heatmap with zoom and country drilldown
scenario_context: Customer-distribution map by country. Click country to see cities. Need: registered map data, zoom, drilldown state.
verification_chain: Layer 6: /geo/customers returns counts by country/city
Layer 8: chart registers world map JSON
Layer 9: click country → drilldown to its cities
Layer 10: geo_drill_total{country}
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Bundling all country JSON — bloats bundle
No way back to world view
Hardcoded color stops — no legend
Drilldown loses zoom state
what_breaks_naive_approach: 100MB of map data bundled. Lazy-load per-country JSON when needed.
reasoning: World map registered initially. Click handler fetches country GeoJSON + drills. Back button restores world view. visualMap legend explains colors.
shared_schema: // shared/schemas/geo.ts\nimport { z } from 'zod';\nexport const GeoPointSchema = z.object({ name: z.string(), value: z.number() });

error_handling_strategy.client_error_mapping: Show toast 'Detail not available for X'

edge_cases_addressed: Country JSON missing — silent
Empty data — neutral fill
Roam (pan/zoom) — state preserved on drill
Mobile — touch pan/zoom
Theme — light/dark
Country borders disputed — render as data shows
Performance — registerMap once
test_strategy: Storybook with mock data. Click drills.
what_distinguishes_senior_thinking: 1) Lazy map registration — small bundle. 2) Back state explicit. 3) Tooltip teaches data.

title: Tremor metric card with delta, sparkline, and screen-reader summary
scenario_context: KPI cards across dashboard. Each shows current value, % change, and tiny trend sparkline. Must be readable + accessible.
verification_chain: Layer 8: Tremor Card + Metric + AreaChart components
Layer 8: figcaption with summary for screen reader
Layer 9: BadgeDelta colored correctly for direction
Layer 10: card_view_total
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Sparkline without aria — meaningless to screen readers
Up arrow always green — bad when 'errors' is the metric
Sparkline axis visible — too cluttered
No previous value — can't show delta
what_breaks_naive_approach: Errors metric trending UP shown in green (more is bad). Need direction-of-good per metric.
reasoning: Card takes metric + previous + goodDirection ('up' or 'down'). BadgeDelta deltaType picks color. Sparkline minimal (no axes, no grid). figcaption summarizes for SR.
backend_solution: // N/A — extends e2e-028
edge_cases_addressed: No previous value — no delta badge
Zero previous — division by zero → no delta
goodDirection flip — colors swap
Empty series — no sparkline
Single point — flat sparkline
Reduced motion — animations off
Print — clean layout
test_strategy: Storybook combinations: each goodDirection + sign of delta.
what_distinguishes_senior_thinking: 1) goodDirection semantic. 2) figcaption for SR. 3) Sparkline minimalism.

title: Real-time chart updates without breaking user's hover/zoom interaction
scenario_context: Live dashboard updates every 1s. User hovers tooltip — update wipes it. User zoomed in — update resets zoom. Need: preserve interaction state across updates.
verification_chain: Layer 7: chart instance held in ref; updates merge
Layer 8: pause updates while hovering
Layer 8: preserve zoom across updates
Layer 9: clear visual cue when paused
Layer 10: paused_during_interaction_total
anti_patterns_to_avoid: setOption with notMerge:true on every update — resets everything
Replacing dataset wholesale — loses zoom
Auto-resume zoom — confusing
No pause indicator
what_breaks_naive_approach: User tries to read tooltip; update kills it; tries again; fails again. Pause-on-hover preserves interaction.
reasoning: Track hover state via mouseover/mouseout. While hovering, queue updates. On mouseout, apply queued + future updates. Preserve zoom via dataZoom getOption + restore. Show 'paused' indicator while interacting.
backend_solution: // N/A — streaming
edge_cases_addressed: Long hover (30s) — queue grows; coalesce by setOption merge
Disconnect during hover — queued items lost on reconnect
Touch devices — no hover; tap to pin/unpin
Zoom while hover — preserved
Resize during hover — chart resizes
Tab hidden — pause stream too
Multiple charts — each independent
test_strategy: Mock EventSource; hover, assert no setOption fires; mouseout fires merged.
what_distinguishes_senior_thinking: 1) Pause-on-hover respects user. 2) Zoom preserved across updates. 3) Visual indicator — user knows why chart is stale.

title: Refactor API response shape with zero-downtime: shadow read + dual-write
scenario_context: Renaming `displayName` → `fullName` in user response. Need: backward compat during migration, telemetry, gradual rollout, clean cutover.
verification_chain: Layer 5: response includes both fields during migration window
Layer 6: client SDK aliases
Layer 10: legacy_field_read_total
Process: write old + new → read tracks usage → remove old after zero usage
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Hard rename and hope — breaks integrations
Forever supporting both — codebase rot
No telemetry — guess when to remove
Different fields for read vs write
what_breaks_naive_approach: Rename, deploy. External integration breaks. Customer escalation.
reasoning: Phase 1: response includes both, server proxy tracks reads of old. Phase 2: communicate deprecation; SDK auto-uses new. Phase 3: when reads = 0 for N days, remove old in next major version.
shared_schema: // shared/schemas/user.v1.ts vs user.v2.ts; mediator schema for transition
frontend_solution: // see e2e-029
edge_cases_addressed: External integrations — long lead notice
Caching of responses — short TTL during transition
Mobile apps — version-gated; old apps stay supported
Webhooks — same dual-emit
SDK release cadence — phased
Documentation — both shown during transition
Migration window — 90 days typical
test_strategy: Snapshot test of both shapes during transition. After cutover, old absent.
what_distinguishes_senior_thinking: 1) Dual-emit + telemetry > hope. 2) Proxy auto-instrumentation. 3) Cutover gated on data not calendar.

title: What if response is malformed: schema-validated boundaries with fallback UX
scenario_context: Backend deploy breaks response shape. Client code blindly trusts shape, crashes. Need: parse at boundary, fall back gracefully if parse fails.
verification_chain: Layer 6: every API response parsed with Zod
Layer 8: parse failure triggers error UI not crash
Layer 10: api_response_parse_failed_total{endpoint, error}
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Trusting TS types from hand-written interfaces — TS doesn't validate runtime
Object access (data.user.name) without optional chaining
No error boundary above components
Crash silently in production
what_breaks_naive_approach: Server returns user without name field due to a bug. Client crashes throughout. Schema parse catches it: shows 'Couldn't load' instead.
reasoning: Every fetch parses with Zod schema. Parse error → throw to error boundary or set error state. Sentry captures with the malformed payload (scrubbed). Component shows fallback UX. App stays usable.
backend_solution: // see e2e-005

error_handling_strategy.server_errors: Schema mismatch is a server bug — alert immediately
error_handling_strategy.user_facing_messages: 'Something looks off — refresh or contact support'

edge_cases_addressed: Partial response (truncated network) — JSON parse fails first
Wrong content-type — JSON parse fails
Schema added new required field server side — client schema needs update
Server returns array vs object — type mismatch caught
Null vs missing — explicit in schema
Deep validation — caught at root
HTML error page returned by proxy — caught
test_strategy: Inject malformed response, assert ResponseShapeError + fallback UI shown.
what_distinguishes_senior_thinking: 1) Runtime validation at boundary > compile-time only. 2) Sentry context includes sample for debugging. 3) Fallback UX over crash.

title: What if browser refreshes mid-submission: idempotent save + recovery hint
scenario_context: User on long form, refreshes by accident during submit. Naive: form blank, submission state unknown.
verification_chain: Layer 3: idempotent submission via idempotency key
Layer 7: form draft saved to localStorage
Layer 9: on load, detect orphaned in-flight submission, offer recovery
Layer 10: refresh_during_submit_total
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Lost work after refresh
Duplicate submit on retry
No way to verify whether server received
Auto-resubmit silently — surprise
what_breaks_naive_approach: User refreshes mid-submit. Reopens form blank. Submits again. Two records.
reasoning: Form draft persists per field change to localStorage. Submit assigns idempotency key, stored in localStorage. On refresh: check key, ask server if processed. If yes → success. If no → restore draft + offer resubmit.
backend_solution: // see e2e-014 idempotency middleware

error_handling_strategy.recovery_actions: Retry with same key — server dedupes

edge_cases_addressed: Refresh mid-submit — recovery banner
Server processed but response lost — status returns processed
User abandons form for days — draft persists
Multiple devices — draft local only (could sync)
Schema change to form — Zod parse handles old draft
Sensitive data in draft — exclude per-field
Browser cleared cache — start fresh
test_strategy: Cypress: fill form, simulate refresh, see recovery banner, click retry, single record created.
observability_hooks: Recovery banner shown rate — UX signal
what_distinguishes_senior_thinking: 1) Persistent draft survives refresh. 2) Idempotency key + status endpoint catches lost responses. 3) User chooses to retry — no surprise auto-submit.

title: Narrowing patterns: Result<T, E> across layers without exceptions
scenario_context: Mixing thrown errors with returned values makes call sites awkward. Result<T, E> type with .isOk() narrows TS naturally.
verification_chain: Layer 3: service returns Result, not throws for expected errors
Layer 4: controller maps Result to HTTP
Layer 6: client SDK exposes Result for typed handling
Layer 7: state branches on result type
Layer 10: result_outcome_total{ok|err}
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Throwing for control flow (validation, business rules)
Returning {error?, data?} both nullable — confusing
Async functions that throw and return both
Catching everything generically
what_breaks_naive_approach: Service throws QuotaError. Caller forgets to catch. 500 returned for what should be 402.
reasoning: Result<T, E> as discriminated union {ok: true, value} | {ok: false, error}. Services return Results for expected business errors; reserve throw for unexpected (DB down). Controllers map Err to HTTP. Client uses Result for explicit UI branches.

error_handling_strategy.server_errors: Expected → Result.err; unexpected → throw → errorHandler middleware
error_handling_strategy.client_error_mapping: FetchError still used at network boundary

edge_cases_addressed: Async chains — andThen helper for chaining
Async/await mixed — await result.value if ok
Tests assert ok vs err — exhaustive
Migration — wrap throw-based services in Result
Logging — record both ok and err codes
TypeScript exhaustiveness on .error.code
Result.map for transformations
test_strategy: Service unit: assert .ok and .error.code per branch.
what_distinguishes_senior_thinking: 1) Throw for exceptional, Result for expected — semantic clarity. 2) Exhaustive switch on error code. 3) No surprise 500s for business cases.

title: Tenant-scoped configuration with no code branches
scenario_context: Per-tenant feature flags, branding, custom domain. Naive: if (tenantId === 'X') throughout code. Need: tenant-scoped config served from one source.
verification_chain: Layer 1: tenant_config table keyed by (tenantId, configKey)
Layer 3: configService.get(key) returns tenant-scoped or default
Layer 4: middleware seeds tenant config into context
Layer 7: client requests /me/config
Layer 8: feature gates use config.featureFlag('X')
Layer 10: feature_flag_evaluation_total
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Hardcoded tenant ID checks
Single global config
No defaults — every tenant must set
Feature flags without targeting — all-or-nothing
what_breaks_naive_approach: if (tenant.id === 'acme') showBetaFeature — refactor away from this. Use config.featureFlags.includes('beta-feature').
reasoning: Config table is (tenantId, key, value). Service returns tenant value or falls back to default. Cached per-tenant in memory with invalidation event. Feature flag system: a flag returns boolean per tenant. UI hook useFeatureFlag('x') gates conditionally.
edge_cases_addressed: Config change in admin UI — invalidate cache; clients re-fetch on focus
Tenant has no config — defaults
Boolean vs string — Zod schema per key
Migration — backfill defaults
A/B test — flag value is random per user (separate system)
Per-environment config — env override
Compliance restrictions — region-based config
test_strategy: Unit: get returns default if absent; cache hit. Integration: change config, second request sees new.
what_distinguishes_senior_thinking: 1) Code is tenant-agnostic — branches via config. 2) Cache with explicit invalidation. 3) Feature flags decouple deploy from release.

title: Dunning emails for failed payments with attempt count and grace handling
scenario_context: Card declines. Without dunning, customer drops off. Stripe Smart Retries help but customer needs explicit communication and self-service update.
verification_chain: Layer 3: invoice.payment_failed webhook triggers dunning
Layer 3: email queue with attempt-aware templates
Layer 4: /billing/payment-method update endpoint
Layer 7: in-app banner during past_due
Layer 10: dunning_sent_total{attempt}, dunning_recovered_total
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Single dunning email — most don't see first
Dunning after Stripe gives up — too late
Generic 'payment failed' — no recovery action
Cancelling immediately — losing recoverable customers
what_breaks_naive_approach: Card expires. Stripe retries 4 times silently. After 7 days, cancels. Customer never knew. Churn.
reasoning: On payment_failed webhook: count attempts, send templated email with update link. Day 1: gentle. Day 3: clearer. Day 7: final warning. In-app banner persistent. Stripe Smart Retries handles backend; we handle communication.
shared_schema: // shared/schemas/dunning.ts\nimport { z } from 'zod';\nexport const DunningAttemptSchema = z.object({ attemptNumber: z.number(), sentAt: z.coerce.date(), template: z.enum(['gentle','reminder','final']) });

error_handling_strategy.server_errors: Stripe API failure → retry job; email failure → retry
error_handling_strategy.user_facing_messages: Email + in-app banner + modal on critical

edge_cases_addressed: Email bounces — escalate to in-app + admin
Stripe webhook delayed — eventual consistency
Card updated but next charge still pending — show pending state
Customer in different timezone — schedule per their TZ
Duplicate webhook — idempotent via attempt count
Cancelled then recovered — replay attempt 1 next time
Bank holds — extra grace
test_strategy: Mock webhook events, assert email sent with correct template at each attempt. Recovered triggers recovery email + clears dunning.
observability_hooks: Dunning funnel: sent → opened → clicked → recovered. Per-stage drop.
what_distinguishes_senior_thinking: 1) Multi-touch dunning > single email. 2) Recovery email closes the loop. 3) In-app + email + modal — different surfaces catch different users.

title: Granular feature flags per tenant with rollout percentage
scenario_context: Rolling out beta feature. Want to enable for 10% of customers initially. Naive: random per-request — same user sees flag flip. Need: sticky per-tenant assignment.
verification_chain: Layer 1: feature_flags table with target rules
Layer 3: flagService.isEnabled(flag, tenantId) sticky via hash
Layer 4: middleware computes flags into request
Layer 7: client receives flags in /me/config
Layer 8: <Feature> gates UI
Layer 10: feature_evaluation_total{flag, result}
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Math.random — flag flips per evaluation
Storing per-user override — table grows huge
No kill switch — bad flag breaks tenants
Flag check in deep code — coupling
what_breaks_naive_approach: Random rollout: same user gets feature one session, not next. Confusing.
reasoning: Hash(tenantId + flag_name) → bucket 0-99. If bucket < rolloutPercent, enabled. Deterministic per tenant. Server can add explicit allow/deny list. Killswitch sets rolloutPercent=0 instantly via admin endpoint.

error_handling_strategy.server_errors: Cache miss → DB fetch; DB failure → default false

edge_cases_addressed: Killed flag returns false immediately
Allow list override — VIP customers always on
New rollout % — re-hashed assignments stable per tenant
Multi-tenant user impersonation — uses target tenant
Concurrent flag eval — single in-flight load
Cache invalidation cross-instance — pub/sub
Default off — safe default
test_strategy: Hash distribution test (chi-square). Kill switch flips immediately. Allow/deny precedence.
what_distinguishes_senior_thinking: 1) Sticky per-tenant — UX consistent. 2) Kill switch with cache invalidation. 3) Allow/deny lists for VIPs and exclusions.

title: Recharts dashboard with brush for date range selection
scenario_context: Time-series chart with overview brush below for selecting a range. Brushed range filters detail chart + propagates to other charts via context.
verification_chain: Layer 7: brush state in shared filter context
Layer 8: Recharts Brush component
Layer 9: drag handles update range
Layer 10: brush_change_total
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Brush state local — other charts can't sync
Brush updates re-render entire dashboard each frame — throttle
No reset
Brushed range exceeds data — graceful clamp
what_breaks_naive_approach: Brush updates per pixel of drag → 60 setState/sec → janky.
reasoning: Brush component on overview chart. onChange fires with start/end index. Debounce, then update filter context. Detail charts react to filter. Reset clears.
backend_solution: // N/A — same series
edge_cases_addressed: Single point — brush disabled
Mobile touch — works via Recharts
Reset clears filter
Sync across multiple charts
Brush range smaller than min — clamp
Keyboard nav — Recharts limited; provide explicit date inputs as fallback
Reduced motion — instant updates
test_strategy: Mock data; brush, assert filter context updated.
what_distinguishes_senior_thinking: 1) Debounced brush — no jank. 2) Shared filter context — cross-chart sync. 3) Date inputs as keyboard alternative.

title: Null/missing data interpolation strategy: connect, gap, or zero
scenario_context: Time-series has gaps (sensor offline, no data day). Strategy matters: connecting nulls implies continuity that didn't exist; treating as zero implies measurement of zero; gap is most honest but requires user understanding.
verification_chain: Layer 3: API returns explicit null vs 0
Layer 8: chart prop controls strategy: connectNulls vs gap vs zero
Layer 8: legend/tooltip notes nulls
Layer 10: null_strategy_used_total
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Connecting nulls silently — misleading
Treating null as 0 silently — wrong analysis
Gap with no explanation — user confused
Different defaults per chart — inconsistent
what_breaks_naive_approach: Sensor offline for 2 days → chart connects across, suggests steady value. User makes wrong decision.
reasoning: Default to gap (connectNulls={false}). Legend or annotation explains gap. Provide UI toggle for interpolation only when user explicitly requests. Tooltip skips null rows.
shared_schema: // shared/schemas/timeseriesWithNulls.ts\nimport { z } from 'zod';\nexport const NullablePointSchema = z.object({ t: z.coerce.date(), value: z.number().nullable() });
edge_cases_addressed: All nulls — empty state
Single null in middle — small gap visualized
Trailing nulls (no recent data) — show 'data delayed'
Sparse (mostly null) — different empty state
Aggregated to coarser bucket — fewer nulls (data summed)
Forecast vs actual — different line style
Multiple series with different gap patterns
test_strategy: Property: random null patterns, gaps detected.
what_distinguishes_senior_thinking: 1) Gaps visualized, not hidden. 2) Server returns explicit null. 3) UI educates user on data quality.

title: Adding new permission: server check + UI gate + permission map endpoint
scenario_context: Add 'analytics:export' permission. Touch all layers: ROLE_GRANTS, service check, UI gate, /me/permissions endpoint that includes it.
verification_chain: Layer 0: ROLE_GRANTS shared map updated
Layer 3: service for export checks authorize('analytics:export')
Layer 4: route uses checkPermission middleware
Layer 5: /me/permissions returns updated map
Layer 8: <Can do='analytics:export'> in UI
Layer 10: export attempts logged
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Adding only client-side check
Adding only server check (UI still shows button)
Hardcoded permission strings — typo wastes hours
what_breaks_naive_approach: Add UI gate only — savvy user crafts API request, exports anyway.
reasoning: Shared TS const ROLE_GRANTS. Type literal union for compile-time safety. Adding permission requires updating ROLE_GRANTS in shared package. Server's authorize checks, UI's Can checks. Both wired via same constant.
edge_cases_addressed: Permission added mid-session — focus refresh
Role change requires perm refetch
UI test fails if perm not in ROLE_GRANTS
TS literal union catches typos
Migration: existing users get perm based on role
Multi-product permission split
Audit log for sensitive perm changes
test_strategy: Add perm; assert ROLE_GRANTS updated; UI shows for admin only; server denies viewer.
what_distinguishes_senior_thinking: 1) Single source of truth ROLE_GRANTS — typo caught at compile time. 2) Both server + UI gated. 3) Audit trail for sensitive perms.
